coredata.dc:title: Bionanosensor based on N-doped graphene quantum dots coupled with CoOOH nanosheets and their application for in vivo analysis of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chunxia
Pan, Caiwen
Wei, Zitong
Wei, Xiaoran
Yang, Fan
Mao, Lanqun
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we employ 3D nitrogen-doped porous graphene frameworks (NPG) as raw material to prepare emissive nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (r-NGQDs) via chemical oxidation method. The as-prepared fluorescent r-NGQDs was integrated with CoOOH nanosheets to construct a sensing platform for in vivo ascorbic acid (AA) analysis. Initially, the fluorescence emission intensity of r-NGQDs was quenched by CoOOH nanosheets based on the inner filter effect (IFE). Then the quenched intensity of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets system was enlightened by addition of AA, since AA could consume CoOOH nanosheets through redox reaction, leading to the release of r-NGQDs and fluorescence restoration. Moreover, the restored fluorescence intensity of r-NGQDs is highly dependent on the concentration of AA which endows them as a quantitative analysis of AA with a limit of detection (LOD) reach up to1.85 μM (n = 3) in aqueous solution. Finally, the as constructed bionanosensor was further employed for in vivo analysis of AA in living rat brain microdialysate with basal value up to 9.4 ± 1.4 μM (n = 3).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped porous graphene
Graphene quantum dots
Ascorbic acid
CoOOH nanosheets
In vivo

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing for AA based on r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets
In vivo microdialysis and analysis for AA
Characterization of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets
Fluorescence response of CoOOH nanosheets towards r-NGQDs
Fluorometric sensing of AA in rat brain microdialysate

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21804319 , 21776302 , and 21776308 ), the Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum (Beijing) (Nos. 2462016YJRC027 , 2462017BJB04 , 2462015YQ0306 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of r-NGQDs. Inset, size distribution (right). (B) AFM image of r-NGQDs. Inset, height distribution (right).
FT-IR spectra of NPG (black curve), and r-NGQDs (blue curve). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) (B) (C) (D) are XPS survey spectrum, high-resolution C1s, N 1s and O1s XPS of NPG, respectively. (E) (F) (G) (H) are XPS survey spectrum, high-resolution C1s, N 1s and O1s XPS of r-NGQDs, respectively.

(A) Normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra (red curve), excitation (blue curve) and emission (black curve) spectra of r-NGQDs. Inset, the photographs of the r-NGQDs under UV light at 365 nm (left) and day light (right). (B) PL spectra of r-NGQDs under different excitation wavelength spectra from 310 to 510 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of r-NGQDs toward different amount of CoOOH nanosheets. (From top to bottom: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 μg/mL). (B) Plot of F/F 0 versus the amount of CoOOH nanosheets. Inset, calibration curve of CoOOH nanosheets versus F/F 0. F 0 and F denote the PL intensity of r-NGQDs in the absence and presence of CoOOH nanosheets, respectively.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of r-NGQDs, (b) r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets mixture, and (c) AA added to the mixture of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets. Excitation: 330 nm, emission 445 nm.

Plot of F/F 0 of r-NGQDs versus the concentration of CoOOH nanosheets (30, 35, 40, and 50 μg/mL, red column), plot of F′/F 0 of r-NGQDs versus different concentration of CoOOH nanosheets after the addition of ascorbic acid (100 μM, gray column). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of r-NGQDs in the presence of CoOOH nanosheets (35 μg/mL) toward various concentration of AA (from top to bottom: 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM, respectively). (B) Plot of F′ /F versus the concentration of AA. F and F′ represent the PL intensity of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets (35 μg/mL) in the absence and presence of AA, respectively.
Plot of F′/F -1 of r-NGQDs against various metal ions, amino acids and biological substances. The concentrations of amino acids, metal ions, and biological substances are 50 μM, K+ and Na+ are 1 mM.

Fluorescence spectra of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets (black curve) with the addition of aCSF suspension (red curve), and with the addition AAOx solution (blue curve). Excitation: 330 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation of r-NGQDs and in vivo sensing of AA in rat brain microdialysate coupled with CoOOH nanosheets.

head.ce:title: Bionanosensor based on N-doped graphene quantum dots coupled with CoOOH nanosheets and their application for in vivo analysis of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of New Energy and Materials, China University of Petroleum (Beijing), Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
College of New Energy and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of New Energy and Materials, China University of Petroleum (Beijing), Beijing, 102249, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, The Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences
Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, The Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100190, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we employ 3D nitrogen-doped porous graphene frameworks (NPG) as raw material to prepare emissive nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (r-NGQDs) via chemical oxidation method. The as-prepared fluorescent r-NGQDs was integrated with CoOOH nanosheets to construct a sensing platform for in vivo ascorbic acid (AA) analysis. Initially, the fluorescence emission intensity of r-NGQDs was quenched by CoOOH nanosheets based on the inner filter effect (IFE). Then the quenched intensity of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets system was enlightened by addition of AA, since AA could consume CoOOH nanosheets through redox reaction, leading to the release of r-NGQDs and fluorescence restoration. Moreover, the restored fluorescence intensity of r-NGQDs is highly dependent on the concentration of AA which endows them as a quantitative analysis of AA with a limit of detection (LOD) reach up to1.85 μM (n = 3) in aqueous solution. Finally, the as constructed bionanosensor was further employed for in vivo analysis of AA in living rat brain microdialysate with basal value up to 9.4 ± 1.4 μM (n = 3).

Schematic illustration of the preparation of r-NGQDs and in vivo sensing of AA in rat brain microdialysate coupled with CoOOH nanosheets.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) serves as a category of neurotransmitter with multifunctional physiological functions [1]. Besides, AA is an antioxidant with strong ability to clear up free radical, and particularly it is neuroprotective. Moreover, AA plays important role in the central neural system since it acts as a neuromodulator and dominates of both dopamine- and glutamate-mediated neurotransmission [2]. Up to now, many approaches especially electrochemical method have been considered as one of the most commonly used approaches for AA determination [3–6]. However, the electro-oxidation of AA under electrochemical circumstance is irreversible and the oxidized product are prone to attach onto the surface of electrode and contaminate the reacted surface, resulting in high over potential for AA [7,8]. In addition, uric acid (UA) and dopamine (DA) offer electroactive property and could produce similar redox potential analogous to AA and bring about overlaid peaks with each other, causing poor reproducibility and specificity. To eliminate the interference effect of these two electroactive species and achieve the selective detection of AA in the coexisting conditions has attracted much attention [9,10]. Among them, fluorescent approaches for the sensing of AA with high selectivity based on fluorescent quantum dots and nanoparticles have been reported [11,12]. However, some of them suffer from high toxicity of inorganic quantum dots, complex synthesis procedures, and low sensitivity and selectivity. To this purpose, it is highly desirable and has become a challenging research topic to develop a nontoxic, simple method for the analysis of AA with high selectivity and sensitivity (see Scheme 1 ).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), especially nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are attracting tremendous attention owing to their excellent tunable photoluminescence (PL), high quantum yield (QY), low cytotoxicity, and rich surface chemistry [13–15]. Moreover, chemical doping of GQDs could effectively alter their electronic structure, tailor their band-gap, surface and chemical features, and even provide many more edge active sites, therefore inducing new phenomena and performances [16–18]. For instance, since C and N share with the similar atomic size and valence structure, theoretical calculations and experimental fronts demonstrate that doping of GQDs with nitrogen atoms to form C–N bonds could effectively tune/tailor the band-gap of GQDs and introduce new properties [19–22]. In addition, the morphology of raw materials such as porous structure or sheets like structure offers more edge sites also produce new phenomena and unexpected properties [23]. Moreover, we note that the luminescent property of N-GQDs could be suppressed by nanoquencher based on the inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), therefore providing opportunities in fluorescent biosensing.

In this study, r-NGQDs derived from 3D NPG and CoOOH nanosheets were employed for the selective sensing of AA in rat brain microdialysate. The sensing mechanism was based on the IFE between r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets, and the subsequent redox reaction between AA and CoOOH nanosheets. The emission intensity of r-NGQDs was effectively suppressed upon the addition of CoOOH nanosheets. However, the quenched intensity of r-NGQDs could be enlightened by the introduction of AA since AA could decompose CoOOH nanosheets, resulting in the release r-NGQDs accompany with fluorescence recovery. The recovered intensity was highly dependent on the amount of AA which could realize quantitative analysis of AA. The above constructed strategy were then employed as a bionanosensor for the sensitive analysis of AA ranging from 0.5 to 100 μM with LOD reach up to 1.85 μM. Besides, the present assay was successfully employed for the determination of AA in rat brain microdialysate. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report that demonstrate a fluorescent approach for the analysis of AA based on the facile preparation of r-NGQDs from nitrogen doped porous graphene integrated with CoOOH nanosheets.

3D NPG used in this study (Fig. 1 ) were synthesized according to previous report [24]. H2SO4 (98%), HNO3 (68%), H2O2 (30%), HCl (37%), KMnO4, KOH, and KCl were all obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF) was prepared by adding NaCl (126 mM), NaHCO3 (27.5 mM), KCl (2.4 mM), CaCl2 (1.1 mM), MgCl2 (0.85 mM), KH2PO4 (0.5 mM), and Na2SO4 (0.5 mM) into mili-Q water with pH adjusted to 7.3. All the other chemicals and reagents were used without further purifications.

The fluorescence and UV–V is absorption spectra were performed on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The fluorescence decay curves were recorded with FLS980 (Edinburgh, England) fluorescence spectrometer. The morphology of r-NGQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on F-20 microscopy (FEI, USA). The height of r-NGQDs was acquired by Atomic force microscope (AFM) (Bruker, American). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were taken on ESCALab220I-XL. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was conducted on FT-IR Microscope (Frontier IR/FIR, UK).

3D NPG was synthesized via hydrothermal approach with triphenylamine (TPA) as carbon and nitrogen source in a tubular furnace according to previous report [25]. Briefly, active MgO and TPA were dissolved in ethanol and then dried overnight. After that, MgO and TPA were annealed at 900 °C for 2h under argon atmosphere. After cooled to room temperature, dilute hydrochloric acid was used to remove MgO template. Finally, the obtained black solid was dried under vacuum at 80 °C overnight to gain the final 3D NPG.

r-NGQDs was synthesized from nitrogen doped porous graphene by acid oxidation method [26]. Typically, 20 mg of NPG, 20 mg of KMnO4, 2 mL of HNO3, and 10 mL of concentrated H2SO4 was added into the reaction tube and heated to 80 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the light yellow aqueous solution was neutralized, and 100 mg of NaBH4 was added to the above mixture under ice condition for another 4 h. Finally, the obtained suspension was concentrated and dialyzed (MWCO, 500–1000 Da) for 10 days to give the final product (r-NGQDs) and used for further analysis.

1 mL of NaClO (0.2 M) and 1 mL of NaOH (0.8 M) were mixed with 2 mL of CoCl2 (10 mM) solution and sonicated for 10 min. Then, the brown solid product was centrifuged, washed, and dried under vacuum. The final product was dispersed in mili-Q water for further use.

The excitation dependent photoluminescence of r-NGQDs aqueous solution was recorded under different excitation wavelength (310 nm–510 nm). As for the pH dependent photoluminescence, the pH of r-NGQDs suspension was changed from 2 to 12 for instant fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing for AA based on r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, different concentrations of CoOOH nanosheets were added to the r-NGQDs solutions to form a stock solution of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets mixture. Then, different concentrations (0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM) of AA added into 1.0 mL of fresh r-NGQDs/CoOOH nanosheets solution and allowed to keep 2 min at room temperature before measurement. The fluorescence intensities of r-NGQDs were monitored before and after the addition of AA. To evaluate the selectivity of this fluorescent nanosensor in biological samples, all the possible interference species including metal ions and small biomolecules were added into r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets stock solution separately in a similar way as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo microdialysis and analysis for AA
ce:section.ce:para: In vivo microdialysis was conducted in accordance with previous study [27]. In brief, Sprague−Dawley rats (370–400 g) with guide cannula were anaesthetized with pentobarbital (345 mg kg−1, i.p.) after they were fixed carefully. Then, the microdialysis guide cannulas were inserted into the striatum and fixed. After 24 h recovery, we insert a microdialysis probe into rat striatum and then perfuse with artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF). After reaching equilibrium for 1.5 h by perfusing aCSF, the microdialysate was collected each 30 μL for instant fluorescent analysis.

For fluorescent analysis of AA in the brain microdialysate of rat striatum, a stock solution of r-NGQDs, CoOOH nanosheets (35 μg/mL) and pyrophosphate ion (PPi, 100 μM) was prepared and corresponding response was recorded. Then, the microdialysate (30 μL) solution was added into the above 1 mL of stock solution to form mixture A and allowed to standby for 10 min before fluorescence measurement. At the same time, another mixture B containing microdialysate (30 μL) and AAOx (250 U/mL) was added into 1 mL of stock solution and monitored by the same procedure. Each experiment was carried out three times dependently. By subtracting the maximum fluorescence intensity of mixture A and AAOx-treated mixture B, the amount of AA in the brain microdialysate can be calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the as-synthesized r-NGQDs was characterized by TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 1A, the r-NGQDs is uniformly dispersed spherical particle with an average size distribution of 2.52 nm ± 0.62 nm around 1.5 nm– 4.5 nm, which are consistent with those of N-GQDs obtained electrochemically [28]. The AFM image of r-NGQDs displays a typical height of 0.5–1.0 nm (Fig. 1B), indicating that the as-prepared r-NGQDs possess 1–3 layers of NPG. As for the CoOOH nanosheets, SEM, and TEM were employed to characterize the morphology. Fig. S1 A and S1B display a typical SEM and TEM image of CoOOH nanosheets, showing an obvious hexagonal sheets like structure with latitude ranges from 20 to 40 nm, which is in agreement with the reported CoOOH nanosheets [29]. Moreover, HRTEM image shows a 0.23 nm lattice corresponding to the d spacing of the 012 plane of CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. S1B), confirming the facile synthesis of sheets like CoOOH. Moreover, the XRD spectrum of CoOOH nanosheets demonstrates that the Co2+ was converted into CoOOH nanosheets with three characteristic diffraction peaks located at 19.92°, 39.21°, and 50.87°, corresponding to (003), (012) and (015) of CoOOH nanosheets, respectively (JCPDS: No. 07–0169) (Fig. S2). These values are in agreement with reported literature [30].

The surface functionality of NPG and r-NGQDs were also investigated by FT-IR. In NPG, as depicted in Fig. 2 , the bands located at 3424, and 3200 cm−1 are assigned to the presence of stretching vibration of O–H and N–H vibrations, respectively [31].The peaks at 2920, 2852 cm−1 are ascribed to the C–H vibration and the peaks at 1625, 1463 cm−1 corresponds to the CC and CO stretching vibration from NPG. After chemical oxidation and reduction of NPG to r-NGQDs, the peaks at 1617 and 1433 cm−1 with wavelength shift belong to the CC and CO display an enhanced intensity compared to that of NPG. Moreover, a sharply enhancement peak appeared at 1121 cm−1 accompany with 4 cm−1 redshift in comparison with that of NPG, are ascribed to the C–OH or CH2–OH vibrations, suggesting that the oxidation process produce efficient oxygen-containing hydroxyl groups. While we note that the CC (1617 cm−1) and CO (1433 cm−1) even the N–H vibrations (1339 cm−1) remain after the oxidation process although a slight wavelength shift can be observed, indicating the skeleton of graphene are preserved. In addition, the appearance of new peak at 619 cm−1 belongs to the out of plane bending vibration (β (O–H)) point to the formation of hydroxyl groups, which further confirmed that the oxidation of NPG to r-NGQDs occurs. These surface functional groups of r-NGQDs with abundant hydroxyl groups, oxygen containing groups, and N–H groups endow them with multiple interaction with small molecules. Moreover, the existence of abundant hydroxyl and amino functional groups on the surface of r-NGQDs enable the highly polarity and hydrophilicity and therefore it can be easily dispersed in mili-Q water, enabling the r-NGQDs with rich surface functional groups which is sensitive to various binding.

In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to investigate the element analysis and surface composition of NPG and r-NGQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 , the presence of C, N, and O elements of NPG and r-NGQDs were evidenced by XPS analysis (Fig. 3 A and E). Fig. 3A displays three obvious peaks of C1s at around 285.0 eV, O1s at about 532.0 eV, and N1s at approximate 400.0 eV with an atomic percentage of 35.6%, 27.4% and 1.1% respectively. While these atomic ratio values rise slightly to 36.4%, 29.3% and 1.2% in the corresponding regions of r-NGQDs. The high-resolution C1s spectra of NPG can be decomposed into four peaks (Fig. 3B), belongs to sp2 carbons (CC, 284.4 eV), sp3 carbons (C–C, 284.7 eV), nitrogen carbons (C–N, 285.2 eV), and hydroxyl carbons (C–OH, 286.5 eV) [32]. Besides the above-mentioned category of carbon bonds, carbonyl carbons (CO/C–O, 288.4 eV) can be found in the C1s spectra of r-NGQDs (Fig. 3F), suggesting that the as-prepared r-NGQDs with rich oxygenated surface chemistry. The N1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks which are assigned to pyridinic N (399.5 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2 eV), and graphitic N (401.3 eV), respectively (Fig. 3C and G). At the same time, the O1s spectrum of r-NGQDs are divided into three peaks at around 531.2, 531.9 and 532.6 eV for C–OH, CO, and C–O, respectively (Fig. 3H), revealing that the oxidized porous quantum dots owns an amount of oxygen-containing groups with an atomic value of 29%. Whereas the O1s band of the NPG located at 531.3, 532.0 and 532.7 eV corresponding to the C–OH, CO and C–O, respectively. (Fig. 3D). The presence of oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups provides rich hydroxyl and amino bonds, which therefore could offer various interaction modes with small molecules and induce the change of the luminescence intensity as well as enhance the chelating ability with small molecules.

The optical properties of r-NGQDs were investigated by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 A, r-NGQDs displays a weak absorption in the UV–visible region and negligible peak can be observed, while a strong blue emission peak appears at 419 nm under excitation at 326 nm. We note that r-NGQDs shows light yellow colored aqueous solution with blue fluorescence under radiation at 365 nm (Fig. 4A, inset). Moreover, the photoluminescence of the r-NGQDs displays excitation-dependent fluorescence. With the excitation wavelength shifted from 310 nm to 510 nm, the maximum fluorescence intensity increases from 310 nm to 330 nm excitation wavelength and then decline sharply from 330 nm to 510 nm. Therefore in the following experiments, r-NGQDs with maximum intensity under 330 nm was utilized for fluorescent sensing applications. Moreover, the maximum emission peaks of r-NGQDs red-shifted to longer wavelength with excitation wavelengths increases. Which may be due to the different size distributions and surface defect induced emission, benefiting from inherent structure of NPG in which NPG offers efficient active sites and abundant surface defects. In addition, the relative quantum yield of r-NGQDs was estimated to be about 1.6% in neutral conditions. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54) was selected as a standard reagent. The higher luminescent efficiency of r-NGQDs possibly arises from the reason that r-NGQDs are equipped with abundant aromatic compounds and functional groups as well as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on their surface, leading to enhanced fluorescence. In addition, the r-NGQDs shows a pH sensitive response in aqueous solution (Fig. S3). The PL intensity of r-NGQDs increases with pH increases from 2 to 7, and then decline quickly in alkaline condition with pH varies from 7 to 12. The corresponding phenomenon is probably due to the functional groups such as amino and carboxylic groups of r-NGQDs induced protonation and non-protonation dominate the pH induced fluorescence change. While this pH-dependent result is consistent with many reported fluorescent carbon or graphene quantum dots [11,37]. Since in real samples, neutral physiological environment commonly require a pH range from 7 to 8, the maximum PL intensity under pH = 7 seems applicable for the following biosensing and in vivo analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CoOOH nanosheets towards r-NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CoOOH nanosheets, a type of layered two-dimensional double hydroxide oxidized nanomaterial, which consists of rich functional hydroxyl groups with a good water dispersibility [33,34], providing abundant interaction ways for small molecules. By taking the advantages of these properties, the fluorescent of persistent luminescent nanoparticles, nanodots, and even upconversion nanoparticles have been suppressed by CoOOH nanosheets [35–37]. Moreover, CoOOH nanosheets owns a wide absorption spectra ranging from 300 to 600 nm with a maximum peak at 375 nm, which could overlays with the excitation and emission spectra of most of fluorescent nanomaterials, enabling them serve as an efficient nanoquencher.

On the basis of multiple emission properties of r-NGQDs that owns a wide spectral range from 400 nm to 600 nm which will provide possible overlap with compounds that owns a wide absorption range. As demonstrated in Fig. S4A, r-NGQDs shows exhibit an emission peak at 446 nm (Fig. S4A, black curve) and an excitation peak at 326 nm (Fig. S4A, pink curve), while these two peaks can be overlayed by the whole absorption spectra of CoOOH nanosheets within the range from 300 nm to 600 nm (Fig. S4A, blue curve). Therefore, CoOOH nanosheets could acts as the ideal nanoquencher for emissive r-NGQDs. Besides, UV–V is spectra of CoCl2 (Fig. S4B, pink curve) displays a typical absorption peak at 510 nm which was totally different from CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. S4B, blue curve), suggesting the successful synthesis of CoOOH nanosheets with noninterference from CoCl2.

As depicted in Fig. 5 A, the r-NGQDs exhibits an emission peak located at 445 nm under 330 nm excitation. Upon addition of CoOOH nanosheets, the emission intensity of r-NGQDs was sharply decreased with the increasing amount of CoOOH nanosheets concentration, suggesting that the overlap of the spectra dominate the whole process. The quenching is possibly due to the overlapped spectra between r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets induced IFE since the measured fluorescence life time of r-NGQDs remains almost constant in the absence (Fig. S5, black curve, 6.03167 ns) and presence (Fig. S5, red curve, 6.03122 ns) of CoOOH nanosheets. As we can see from Fig. 5B, the emission intensity ratio (F/F 0) of r-NGQDs shows a linear relationship with CoOOH nanosheets concentration in the lower range from 5 to 15 μg/mL. While upon the continue increasing addition of CoOOH nanosheets, the F/F 0 demonstrate a non-linear curve and almost reached equilibrium when the concentration reached 40 μg/mL. This is probably due to the surface of CoOOH nanosheets may be occupied by the r-NGQDs, even excess amount of CoOOH nanosheets could not produce significant emission variation. In addition, the r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets system was investigated by monitoring the emission intensity under successive irradiation at 330 nm. Fig. S6 shows the emission intensity of r-NGQDs versus time changes upon the addition of CoOOH nanosheets. The emission intensity of the r-NGQDs decreases sharply when CoOOH nanosheets was first added into the system, then the emission intensity keeps almost constant as time increases, indicating that the quenched intensity occurs quickly and then the suppressed system displays high stability.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the above r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets system for further application, we employ the above system for the “on-off-on” detection for reducing molecules. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 , the r-NGQDs displays a strong emission peak at 445 nm (Fig. 6, black curve) under the optimum excitation (330 nm), addition of CoOOH nanosheets result in a significant fluorescence quenching (Fig. 6, red curve). However, the quenched fluorescence of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets system would be enlighted by the addition of AA (Fig. 6, blue curve). With the addition of strong reducing agent AA, CoOOH nanosheets could be reduced to Co2+ and decomposed rapidly, whereas AA was oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid [32], thus r-NGQDs could be released to the solution accompanies with fluorescence recovery. It should be noted that metal ions usually cause quenching effect to carbon dots, herein, the influence of Co2+ on the fluorescence of r-NGQDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the emission intensity of r-NGQDs did not produce an evident suppressed phenomenon on the r-NGQDs with the Co2+ concentration increases from 0 to 100 μM, suggesting that the influence of Co2+ can be negligible.

To acquire the optimum performance for the AA analysis, we investigate the effects of CoOOH nanosheets concentration on the fluorescence recovery of r-NGQDs towards AA. As show in Fig. 7 , different amounts of CoOOH nanosheets were added into the r-NGQDs, and the fluorescence ratio of F/F 0 was employed to estimate the quenching efficiency, in which F 0 denote the fluorescence intensity of r-NGQDs, and F is the fluorescence intensity of r-NGQDs after addition of CoOOH nanosheets. With the increasing amount of CoOOH nanosheets from 30, 35, 40 to 50 μg/mL, the corresponding ratio of F/F 0 decreases from 0.16, 0.12, 0.10 to 0.06 (Fig. 7, red column), suggesting the larger amount of CoOOH nanosheets result in larger quenching effect. When the specific amount of AA (100 μM) was added to the above four mixture, we found out that the ratio of F ′/F was 2.54, 2.97, 2.81, and 1.86 (Fig. 7 gray column), in which F ′ represents the fluorescence intensity of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets after the addition of AA. Although 35 μg/mL of CoOOH nanosheets produce the moderate quenching of r-NGQDs among the tested quencher concentration, but it gives the most excellent fluorescence recovery after the introduction of AA. This is probably because lower amount of CoOOH nanosheets (30 μg/mL) displays inadequate quenching effect towards r-NGQDs since part of r-NGQDs was deposited on the surface of CoOOH nanosheets. whereas moderate amount of CoOOH nanosheets (35 μg/mL) lead to sufficient quenching effect and all of the r-NGQDs was deposited on the surface of CoOOH nanosheets. This speculation has been certified by TEM results, from the TEM image of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. S8), we observed that r-NGQDs (black dots) were successfully deposited on the CoOOH nanosheets surface (hexagonal nanosheets). As for excess amount of CoOOH nanosheets (50 μg/mL) was used as the nanoquencher for r-NGQDs, part of CoOOH nanosheets was occupied by r-NGQDs, leaving unoccupied CoOOH nanosheets in free state in the aqueous solution. Upon addition of AA to the excess amount of CoOOH nanosheets employed system, AA was consumed by the r-NGQDs unoccupied CoOOH nanosheets and r-NGQDs occupied CoOOH nanosheets at the same time, therefore, it could not produce expected fluoresce signal. Therefore, considering the overall performance, in the following experiments, 35 μg/mL of CoOOH nanosheets was utilized as the optimal condition for the quenching experiments. In addition, we investigated the equilibrium time of the AA to the r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets system. As shown in Fig. S9, upon addition of AA to the r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets solution, the fluorescence restore rapidly in 1 min and almost reached equilibrium in 2 min. As for the following experiments, 2 min was employed for incubation of AA with r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets solution.

Since r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets mixture is sensitive to AA. We investigate the performance of as-obtained system for AA analysis. As shown in Fig. 8 A the fluorescence intensity of r-NGQDs increases sharply as the concentration of AA increases. From Fig. 8B we can see that F ′/F shows a linear relationship towards AA in the whole range from 0.5 to 100 μM with the linear regression equation depicted as F ′/F = 0.0183C + 0.986 (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.980, n = 3), where C is the concentration of AA (μM). F and F ′ are the fluorescence intensities of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets at 443 nm in the absence and presence of AA, respectively. According to the three independent experiments, the LOD was estimated to be 1.85 μM based on 3σ/s calculation (σ is the standard deviation of background, s represents the slope of the equation), this value is lower or equivalent compared to most of the reported methods for AA analysis. This result shows that the system response to AA in a quick mode. The recovered fluorescence intensity derives from the AA with a strong reducing property can effective reduce CoOOH nanosheets to Co2+, causing the release of r-NGQDs and fluorescence recovery. During the redox process, the reduction product of Co2+ with an absorption peak of Co2+ at 510 nm, the oxidizing of AA to generate dehydroascorbic acid was both confirmed by UV–Vis spectra and mass spectra [36]. This linear-relationship towards AA in the measured range may arises from the direct reaction between AA and CoOOH nanosheets in which the reaction can proceed in the 1:2 equivalent ratio and can be expressed by the following equation.
2CoOOH nanosheets + AA+4H+ → 2Co2+ + Oxidized AA+4H2O

To explore the possibility of r-NGQDs for sensing AA in complex physically environment, the selectivity of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets towards AA is an important factor since AA would be interference by other active species existed. The selectivity of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets towards various substances and AA were measured in order to obtain a high selectivity for target analyte. The experiments were conducted by adding representative metal ions, biological substances and amino acids, respectively to r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets. The fluorescence ratio of F′/F -1 was employed to monitor the fluorescence intensity variation by adding diverse metal ions (such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mg2+,K+, Na+, Ca2+), amino acids (L-Arg, Gly, GSH, HCy, Cys), and biological substance (UA, Glu, DA, DOPAC), and even all of the above-mixture to the sensing system. As shown in Fig. 9 , the response of this system displays highly selectivity towards AA in comparison with the other species. Moreover, we noticed that most of the metal ions, reducing agent containing molecules such as GSH and Gly did not induce obvious interference. However, we note that Cu2+ or Cu2+ containing mixture produce slight moderate quenching effect on the emission intensity of r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets. We deduce that the quenching probably due to the interaction between Cu2+ and surface functional groups of r-NGQDs, since CoOOH nanosheets and Cu2+ did not react with each other. Apart from Cu2+, Fe3+ have negative effect on r-NGQDs due to interaction of metal ions and the oxygen-containing functional group on the surface of r-NGQDs [38,39]. In this way, in order to minimize the quenching effect on the r-NGQDs, pyrophosphate ion (PPi) with strong binding affinity towards Cu2+ and other metal ions was employed in the following mixture. To certify the PPi masking ability for Fe3+, we measured the r-NGQDs and CoOOH in the absence and presence of Fe3+ and Fe3+/PPi (Fig. S10). We found out that PPi could effectively acts as a masking agent for Fe3+ in the present system. Therefore, by adding PPi to the above system can effectively prevent the interference of Fe3+ and Cu2+. Taken all the above results together, it can be concluded that the r-NGQDs demonstrate an extraordinary high selectivity for AA over these interference substances even under their coexistence conditions. It is inspiring that the r-NGQDs integrated with CoOOH nanosheets system can sense AA under the coexistence of complex biological species, indicating an ultrahigh selectivity of this system for sensing AA in and its potential application in living samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric sensing of AA in rat brain microdialysate
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the prepared r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets can determine AA with highly sensitivity and selectivity, we explored the feasibility of using r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets sensing system for the analysis of AA in practical applications. Moreover, to achieve a high selectivity for AA, ascorbate oxidase (AAOx), a kind of AA oxidizing enzyme that can recognize AA specifically was employed to discriminate the response of AA from the complex system in rat brain such as HCy and Cys. Briefly, a 30 μL aliquot of rat brain microdialysates solution was mixed in the sensing system and allowed to keep standstill for 2 min, another sample composed of 30 μL aliquot of rat brain microdialysates and AAOx was prepared to validate the accurate AA response, and then the fluorescence intensity was recorded subsequently. Upon the addition of microdialysate, the fluorescence intensity of r-NGQDs (Fig. 10 , red curve) increase sharply, while the addition of AAOx containing microdialysate result in the decrease of emission intensity, indicating the above sensing works and AA in rat brain microdialysates can recover the fluorescence of r-NGQDs, while AAOx could effectively consume AA and thus suppress the fluorescence intensity. By subtracting the emission intensity between the two samples with (Fig. 10, blue curve), and without AAOx (Fig. 10, red curve), Based on the linear regression equation described above, the value of AA in rat brain microdialysates was estimated to be 9.4 ± 1.4 μM (n = 3) (Table S1), which agrees well with the value of former literature [40,41]. The specific test values of AA with various methods are summarized in Table S2. Moreover, the present method displays a lower detection limit in comparison with the electrochemical method in vivo sensing of AA in rat brain.

Herein, we synthesize emissive r-NGQDs derived from 3D NPG via chemical oxidation and demonstrate an “off-on” approach based bionanosensor to achieve the quantitative analysis of AA in rat brain microdialysate. The fluorescence off strategy is based on the IFE induced turn off between r-NGQDs and CoOOH nanosheets while the subsequent light on for AA is due to the redox reaction between CoOOH and AA. CoOOH nanosheets acts as the nanoquencher to suppress the emission intensity of r-NGQDs, whereas the quenched intensity of r-NGQDs can be enlightened by the introduction of AA due to AA acts as a reducing agent to decompose CoOOH nanosheets, resulting in the release the r-NGQDs accompany with the lightning of r-NGQDs. The bionanosensor was employed for the quantitative analysis of AA with the LOD up to 1.85 μM. Furthermore, this sensing approach was employed for in vivo analysis of AA in living rat brain, which offers potential applications in psychological applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21804319, 21776302, and 21776308), the Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum (Beijing) (Nos. 2462016YJRC027, 2462017BJB04, 2462015YQ0306).
